---
title: "AI Adoption Prediction: Random Forest"
author: "David Rios"
---


## Motivations

After doing my EDA in the previous document, I was interested in whether we  canpredict which firms adopt AI. Thus, I decided to run a Random Forest.

## Uploading Libraries

```{r, setup, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
library(tidyverse)
library(tidymodels)
library(randomForest)
library(ranger)
library(vip)

tidymodels_prefer()
```


```{r}
## ── 1. Prep the data ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────

rf_data <- tech_2018_clean |>
  filter(
    tech_type == "artificial intelligence",
    used_tech %in% c("Used", "Did not use"),  # drop Unknown
    !is.na(n_emp), !is.na(payroll), !is.na(n_firms),
    n_emp > 0, payroll > 0, n_firms > 0,
    naics_sector != "Total"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    ai_adopted  = as.factor(if_else(used_tech == "Used", "Yes", "No")),
    avg_wage    = payroll / n_emp,
    emp_per_firm = n_emp / n_firms,
    naics_label = as.factor(naics_label),
    state       = as.factor(state)
  ) |>
  select(ai_adopted, n_emp, n_firms, avg_wage, emp_per_firm, 
         payroll, naics_label, state) |>
  drop_na()


## ── 2. Model Specification ─────────────────────────────────────────────────

rf_spec <- rand_forest() |>
  set_mode("classification") |>
  set_engine("ranger") |>
  set_args(
    mtry        = NULL,   # defaults to sqrt(n_predictors)
    trees       = 1000,
    min_n       = 2,
    probability = FALSE,
    importance  = "impurity"  # Gini index for variable importance
  )


## ── 3. Fit the forest ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────

set.seed(253)

ai_forest <- rf_spec |>
  fit(ai_adopted ~ ., data = rf_data)


## ── 4. OOB Error & Confusion Matrix ───────────────────────────────────────

# Overall OOB error rate
ai_forest

#  labels map
var_labels <- c(
  "n_firms"      = "Number of Firms",
  "emp_per_firm" = "Employees per Firm",
  "naics_label"  = "Industry Sector",
  "n_emp"        = "Number of Employees",
  "avg_wage"     = "Average Wage",
  "payroll"      = "Total Payroll",
  "state"        = "State")

## ── 5. Variable Importance ─────────────────────────────────────────────────
ai_forest |>
  extract_fit_engine() |>
  pluck("variable.importance") |>
  enframe(name = "Variable", value = "Importance") |>        # tibble
  mutate(Variable = recode(Variable, !!!var_labels)) |>      # rename
  arrange(Importance) |>
  mutate(Variable = fct_inorder(Variable)) |>                # preserve order
  ggplot(aes(x = Importance, y = Variable)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  labs(
    title    = "What Drives AI Adoption?",
    subtitle = "Variable importance from Random Forest (Gini impurity index)",
    x        = "Importance",
    y        = NULL,
    caption  = "Source: 2018 Annual Business Survey (ABS)"
  )


## ── 6. In-sample confusion matrix ─────────────────────────────────────────

ai_forest |>
  predict(new_data = rf_data) |>
  cbind(rf_data) |>
  conf_mat(
    truth    = ai_adopted,
    estimate = .pred_class
  )
```


## Merging with Company Size

2017 snapshot as pre-adoption context:


```{r}
# Pull 2017 industry-level financials from cs_panel_clean
# ONE row per industry (national average)
industry_financials <- cs_panel_clean |>
  filter(year == 2017) |>
  group_by(naics_label) |>
  summarise(
    revenue_per_firm = mean(revenue_per_firm, na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_wage_panel   = mean(avg_wage,         na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# Rebuild rf_data with the new variables
rf_data <- tech_2018_clean |>
  filter(
    tech_type == "artificial intelligence",
    used_tech %in% c("Used", "Did not use"),
    !is.na(n_emp), !is.na(payroll), !is.na(n_firms),
    n_emp > 0, payroll > 0, n_firms > 0,
    naics_sector != "Total"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    ai_adopted   = as.factor(if_else(used_tech == "Used", "Yes", "No")),
    avg_wage     = payroll / n_emp,
    emp_per_firm = n_emp / n_firms,
    naics_label  = as.factor(naics_label),
    state        = as.factor(state)
  ) |>
  left_join(industry_financials, by = "naics_label") |>   # ← merge here
  select(
    ai_adopted, n_emp, n_firms, avg_wage, emp_per_firm,
    payroll, naics_label, state,
    revenue_per_firm, avg_wage_panel                       # ← new vars
  ) |>
  drop_na()

# Update labels map to include new variables
var_labels <- c(
  "n_firms"          = "Number of Firms",
  "emp_per_firm"     = "Employees per Firm",
  "naics_label"      = "Industry Sector",
  "n_emp"            = "Number of Employees",
  "avg_wage"         = "Average Wage (2018)",
  "payroll"          = "Total Payroll",
  "state"            = "State",
  "revenue_per_firm" = "Revenue per Firm (2017)",
  "avg_wage_panel"   = "Avg. Industry Wage (2017)"
)
```


```{r}
# ── Check the merged data ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
glimpse(rf_data)
# Should now show 9 predictors + ai_adopted, with revenue_per_firm & avg_wage_panel

# ── Re-fit the forest with new data ───────────────────────────────────────
set.seed(253)
ai_forest <- rf_spec |> fit(ai_adopted ~ ., data = rf_data)

# ── 1. OOB Summary ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ai_forest  # check if OOB error changed with new vars

# ── 2. OOB Confusion Matrix ───────────────────────────────────────────────
ai_forest |>
  extract_fit_engine() |>
  pluck("confusion.matrix") |>
  t()

# ── 3. Variable Importance Plot ───────────────────────────────────────────
ai_forest |>
  extract_fit_engine() |>
  pluck("variable.importance") |>
  enframe(name = "Variable", value = "Importance") |>
  mutate(Variable = recode(Variable, !!!var_labels)) |>
  arrange(Importance) |>
  mutate(Variable = fct_inorder(Variable)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = Importance, y = Variable)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  labs(
    title    = "What Drives AI Adoption?",
    subtitle = "Variable importance from Random Forest (Gini impurity index)",
    x        = "Importance (Gini)",
    y        = NULL,
    caption  = "Source: 2018 Annual Business Survey + 2017 Economic Census"
  )

# ── 4. In-sample Confusion Matrix (visualized) ────────────────────────────
ai_forest |>
  predict(new_data = rf_data) |>
  cbind(rf_data) |>
  conf_mat(truth = ai_adopted, estimate = .pred_class) |>
  autoplot(type = "heatmap") +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "white", high = "#2d6a4f") +  # ← green gradient
  labs(
    title    = "In-Sample Confusion Matrix",
    subtitle = "Random Forest: perfect in-sample fit (expected for unpruned trees)",
    caption  = "Note: OOB error (3.09%) is the honest estimate of out-of-sample performance."
  )

# ── 5. Single Example Tree (from the forest) ──────────────────────────────
# Forests can't be plotted directly, so we build ONE tree for illustration
library(rpart)
library(rpart.plot)
single_tree <- decision_tree() |>
  set_mode("classification") |>
  set_engine("rpart") |>
  set_args(cost_complexity = 0.01, tree_depth = 3) |>  # ← shallower
  fit(ai_adopted ~ n_firms + emp_per_firm + avg_wage + 
                   revenue_per_firm + avg_wage_panel,   # ← drop state & naics_label
      data = rf_data)

single_tree |>
  extract_fit_engine() |>
  rpart.plot(
    type        = 4,
    extra       = 104,
    box.palette = "GnRd",
    roundint    = FALSE,    # ← silences the warning
    main        = "Example Decision Tree\n(Simplified — forest uses 1000 of these)"
  )

# ── 6. Classification Regions (2 key vars) ────────────────────────────────
# Visualize how the forest splits on the 2 most important vars
grid <- expand.grid(
  n_firms      = seq(min(rf_data$n_firms), max(rf_data$n_firms), length.out = 150),
  emp_per_firm = seq(min(rf_data$emp_per_firm), max(rf_data$emp_per_firm), length.out = 150)
) |>
  mutate(
    n_emp            = median(rf_data$n_emp),
    avg_wage         = median(rf_data$avg_wage),
    payroll          = median(rf_data$payroll),
    naics_label      = "Manufacturing",   # ← neutral mid-adoption industry
    state            = "06",              # ← California, high adoption state
    revenue_per_firm = median(rf_data$revenue_per_firm, na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_wage_panel   = median(rf_data$avg_wage_panel,   na.rm = TRUE)
  )

ai_forest |>
  predict(new_data = grid) |>
  cbind(grid) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = n_firms, y = emp_per_firm, fill = .pred_class)) +
  geom_tile(alpha = 0.8) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("No" = "#f4a582", "Yes" = "#2d6a4f")) +
  scale_x_log10(labels = scales::comma) +
  scale_y_log10(labels = scales::comma) +
  labs(
    title    = "Forest Classification Regions",
    subtitle = "AI adoption boundary: firm count vs. firm size (Manufacturing, CA)",
    x        = "Number of Firms (log scale)",
    y        = "Employees per Firm (log scale)",
    fill     = "AI Adopted?",
    caption  = "Source: 2018 Annual Business Survey (ABS)\nOther variables held at median values."
  )

```
